---
title: "Bayesian Updating"
author: "Kyle Caudle"
date: "2026-08-24"
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: xelatex
header-includes:
  - |
    \usepackage[most]{tcolorbox}

    % Colors follow the Okabe-Ito / Wong colorblind-safe qualitative palette
    % (Okabe & Ito, 2002; Wong, B. "Points of view: Color blindness."
    % Nature Methods 8, 441 (2011). doi:10.1038/nmeth.1618). These hues were
    % selected to remain distinguishable under all three common forms of
    % color vision deficiency -- unlike the original red/green pairing.
    % See CHANGELOG below for the box-to-color mapping and what's still
    % pending.
    \definecolor{wongorange}{HTML}{E69F00}
    \definecolor{wongblue}{HTML}{0072B2}
    \definecolor{wonggreen}{HTML}{009E73}
    \definecolor{wongvermillion}{HTML}{D55E00}

    \newtcolorbox{keyidea}{
      breakable,
      colback=wongorange!5!white,
      colframe=wongorange!70!black,
      title=Key Takeaway,
      fonttitle=\bfseries,
      boxrule=0.8pt,
      arc=2mm,
      left=2mm,
      right=2mm,
      top=1mm,
      bottom=1mm
    }

    \newtcolorbox{definitionbox}{
      breakable,
      colback=wongblue!7!white,
      colframe=wongblue!65!black,
      title=Definition,
      fonttitle=\bfseries,
      boxrule=0.8pt,
      arc=2mm,
      left=2mm,
      right=2mm,
      top=1mm,
      bottom=1mm
    }

    \newtcolorbox{examplebox}{
      breakable,
      colback=wonggreen!5!white,
      colframe=wonggreen!55!black,
      title=Example,
      fonttitle=\bfseries,
      boxrule=0.8pt,
      arc=2mm,
      left=2mm,
      right=2mm,
      top=1mm,
      bottom=1mm
    }

    \newtcolorbox{warningbox}{
      breakable,
      colback=wongvermillion!4!white,
      colframe=wongvermillion!65!black,
      title=Important Distinction,
      fonttitle=\bfseries,
      boxrule=0.8pt,
      arc=2mm,
      left=2mm,
      right=2mm,
      top=1mm,
      bottom=1mm
    }
---

<!--
CHANGELOG (v02, working copy — cjt)

2026-07-16: Removed the hardcoded Windows path to Anaconda Python in the setup chunk.
  It only resolved on a single machine, so the file couldn't knit elsewhere.
  Replaced with a check for the RETICULATE_PYTHON environment variable,
  which each person sets once in their own local, untracked .Renviron file
  (project root).
2026-07-16: Replaced the hardcoded Windows path to the Bayesian updating
  figure with knitr::include_graphics(here::here("Images", "Bayes_updating.png")).
  here() resolves relative to the project root (wherever math552_labs.Rproj
  lives), so the path works regardless of machine or working directory.
  Requires the `here` package. Image extracted from page 1 of the original pdf.
2026-07-16: Sized down the figure (out.width="75%", centered) — was rendering
  too large in the pdf. Adjust out.width if you want it bigger/smaller.
2026-07-16: Reflowed markdown prose paragraphs to wrap at 80 chars (code
  chunks, YAML, tables, and \begin/\end/\item LaTeX lines were left alone).
  This was a one-time pass, not automatic. To reflow a paragraph you edit
  later:
    RStudio: place cursor in the paragraph, Code > Reflow Comment
      (Cmd+Shift+/ on Mac, Ctrl+Shift+/ on Windows/Linux). Wrap width comes
      from Tools > Global Options > Code > Display > Margin Column (set to
      80).
    Positron: install the "Rewrap" extension (Extensions pane, search
      "Rewrap"; served via Posit's package manager/Open VSX). Set
      rewrap.wrappingColumn to 80 in settings, then place cursor in the
      paragraph and run it (default keybinding Alt+Q). Not yet verified on
      this file's mix of knitr chunks and raw LaTeX boxes — test on one
      paragraph before trusting it on the whole document.

2026-07-16: Replaced all four tcolorbox colors with values from the
  Okabe-Ito / Wong colorblind-safe qualitative palette (Okabe & Ito, 2002;
  Wong, B. "Points of view: Color blindness." Nature Methods 8, 441 (2011).
  doi:10.1038/nmeth.1618). This palette is the standard reference for
  scientific/educational figures because its colors stay distinguishable
  under all three common forms of color vision deficiency, which the
  original red/green pairing did not.
    Key Takeaway            -> #E69F00 (orange)
    Definition              -> #0072B2 (blue)
    Example                 -> #009E73 (bluish green)
    Important Distinction   -> #D55E00 (vermilion)
2026-07-16: Fixed two related errors when knitting in Positron (worked fine
  in RStudio). Root cause for both: R only reads .Renviron from the working
  directory at session *startup*, and Positron's R session appears to start
  in the ThinkBayes2 root rather than math552_labs/ (confirmed via
  here()'s own "here() starts at ..." message pointing at the wrong
  folder). RStudio doesn't hit this because opening math552_labs. Rproj
  directly starts R with math552_labs/ as the working directory.
    (1) RETICULATE_PYTHON not set: fixed pragmatically by adding a second
        .Renviron at the ThinkBayes2 root with the same line as the one in
        math552_labs/. Two files to keep in sync now, not a structural fix.
    (2) here() resolving to the wrong root, so
        here::here("Images", "Bayes_updating.png") pointed at the
        ThinkBayes2 root instead of math552_labs/: initially tried
        here::i_am("math552_lab02_bayesian-updating_v02.Rmd") in the setup
        chunk, on the theory it would pin the root regardless of working
        directory. That was wrong -- i_am() still depends on being able to
        find a project marker by walking up from the working directory, and
        it failed outright ("Could not find associated project") while the
        session's working directory was ThinkBayes2. The actual fix was
        structural: open math552_labs (not ThinkBayes2) as its own
        folder/workspace in Positron, so the R session's working directory
        matches what RStudio was already doing when it opens
        math552_labs.Rproj directly. Once that was fixed, both plain
        here::here() and here::i_am() resolve correctly -- i_am() is being
        kept in the setup chunk since it now works and adds an explicit
        check rather than silent wrong-root resolution, but it was not
        itself the fix.
2026-07-16: POTENTIAL NEXT STEP (not implemented): factor the color/box
  definitions out of each lecture file's header-includes into one shared
  .tex snippet, included by reference, so the box-to-color mapping stays
  consistent across the whole semester's notes instead of being copy-pasted
  (and potentially drifting) file to file.

**AI Usage Notification:** Edits to code were iteratively conducted using Claude
  models in Cowork mode -- cjt retains authorship and responsibility for all
  changes to code and comments.
-->

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(reticulate)
library(here)

# Pin here()'s root to this file's own folder, regardless of which
# directory the R session actually started in (see CHANGELOG below).
here::i_am("math552_lab02_bayesian-updating_v02.Rmd")

# Python interpreter is machine-specific, so it is intentionally NOT
# hardcoded here (see CHANGELOG above). Each user sets RETICULATE_PYTHON
# in their own local .Renviron file in the project root; reticulate reads
# it automatically on startup.
if (Sys.getenv("RETICULATE_PYTHON") == "") {
  stop(
    "RETICULATE_PYTHON is not set.\n",
    "Add this line to a .Renviron file in the project root, then restart R:\n\n",
    "  RETICULATE_PYTHON=/path/to/your/python\n\n",
    "See .Renviron (template) in this folder for an example."
  )
}

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE
)
```

## Bayesian Updating

The focus of this lecture is to show you how to update your prior beliefs as new
information becomes available.  In Bayesian statistics, we no longer treat
probability as a fixed quantity, rather we think of it more as a measure of how
uncertain we are about an unknown parameter or hypothesis.

The following diagram outlines the process.

```{r bayes-updating-fig, echo=FALSE, out.width="75%", fig.align="center"}
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("Images", "Bayes_updating.png"))
```

Our focus with regards to Bayesian updating will be to treat it as an algorithm
rather than a formula. Given a prior distribution and a probabilistic model
describing how the data are generated (the likelihood), we will learn how to
update our beliefs one observation at a time.

\begin{keyidea}
Think of Bayesian updating as a recursive algorithm: today's posterior becomes
tomorrow's prior.
\end{keyidea}


\begin{keyidea}
Bayesian inference is fundamentally an iterative process of learning from
evidence.
\end{keyidea}

\begin{warningbox}
Bayesian updating does not require us to store the entire data set. After each
observation, the posterior distribution summarizes everything learned from all
previous observations. The posterior becomes the prior for the next update.
\end{warningbox}

## Distributions

In probability theory, we define a **distribution** as a set of possible
outcomes and their probabilities.  If the set of outcomes is either *finite* or
*countable* we can describe our distribution with something called a
**probability mass function (PMF)**.

\begin{examplebox}
Consider the flipping of a balanced or fair coin (i.e. Pr(Head)=0.5). The PMF
is:
\end{examplebox}


|Outcome|Prob|
|:--:|:--:|
|Heads|0.5|
|Tails|0.5|

```{python}
from empiricaldist import Pmf

coin = Pmf()
coin['heads'] = 1/2
coin['tails'] = 1/2
print(coin)
```

\begin{examplebox}
A slightly more complicated example is the PMF for the sum obtained when rolling
two fair six-sided dice.
\end{examplebox}


```{python}
from collections import Counter
import pandas as pd

# Generate the sums for all 36 equally likely outcomes
rolls = [d1 + d2
         for d1 in range(1, 7)
         for d2 in range(1, 7)]

# Count occurrences of each sum
counts = Counter(rolls)

# Convert to a PMF
pmf = pd.DataFrame({
    'Count': [counts[s] for s in range(2, 13)],
    'Probability': [counts[s]/36 for s in range(2, 13)]
}, index=range(2, 13))

pmf.index.name = "Sum"

print(pmf)
```


\begin{definitionbox}
A probability mass function (PMF) assigns probabilities to each possible value
of a discrete random variable. The probabilities are nonnegative and sum to one.
\end{definitionbox}

\begin{definitionbox}
The likelihood measures how probable the observed data are under each competing
hypothesis.
\end{definitionbox}

## Cookie Problem (revisited)

\begin{examplebox}
Cookie Bowl Example (Revisited)

\begin{itemize}
\item Bowl 1 contains 75\% vanilla and 25\% chocolate cookies.
\item Bowl 2 contains 50\% vanilla and 50\% chocolate cookies.
\item One bowl is selected at random.
\item Cookies are observed one at a time, updating our beliefs after each observation.
\end{itemize}
\end{examplebox}

```{python}
prior = Pmf.from_seq(['Bowl 1','Bowl 2'])
print(prior)
```

This distribution is called the **prior distribution**.

If we want to update this distribution based on new data (i.e. vanilla cookie
selected), we multiply the prior by the likelihood.

The likelihood of drawing a vanilla cookie from Bowl 1 is 3/4 and the likelihood
of drawing a vanilla cookie from Bowl 2 is 1/2.

```{python}
likelihood_vanilla = [0.75,0.5]
posterior = prior * likelihood_vanilla
print(posterior)
```

Noticed that these probabilities are unnormalized since the probabilities don't
add up to 1.

We can use the function normalize() to normalize the probabilities.

```{python}
posterior.normalize()
```

The multiplication step produces unnormalized posterior weights. Normalization
divides each weight by their total so the probabilities sum to one.  Now when we
print the posterior distribution the probabilities are normalized.

```{python}
print(posterior)
```

With the Pmf object, we are able to update again if more data is received.
Suppose we put the first cookie back and draw again from the same bowl.  Suppose
the second cookie is also vanilla.

The updated posterior distribution would be:

```{python}
posterior *= likelihood_vanilla
posterior.normalize()
print(posterior)
```

\begin{keyidea}
The posterior distribution summarizes everything we have learned from the data
observed so far.

Once the posterior has been computed, the previous observations no longer need
to be stored individually. The posterior becomes the prior for the next
observation, making Bayesian updating a recursive learning algorithm.
\end{keyidea}

Now suppose we draw again and get a chocolate cookie?

```{python}
likelihood_chocolate = [0.25,0.5]
posterior *= likelihood_chocolate
posterior.normalize()
print(posterior)
```

Now, let's take the updating one step further.  Suppose we continue drawing
cookies in the same manner (i.e. with replacement) and keep updating the
posterior distribution.

Rather than updating the posterior manually after each observation, we can
automate the recursive updating process with a simple loop.

\begin{keyidea}
Bayesian updating is a recursive algorithm. At each step, the current posterior
summarizes all previous information and becomes the prior for the next update.
\end{keyidea}

```{python}
from empiricaldist import Pmf
import random
random.seed(8675309)

# -----------------------------
# Prior probabilities
# -----------------------------
prior = Pmf([0.5, 0.5], ["Bowl 1", "Bowl 2"])

# Assume Bowl 1 is the true bowl
n = 100

# Generate a random sequence of observed cookies
cookies = random.choices(
    population=["vanilla", "chocolate"],
    weights=[0.75, 0.25],
    k=n
)

posterior = prior.copy()
history = []

for cookie in cookies:

    if cookie == "vanilla":
        likelihood = {"Bowl 1": 0.75,
                      "Bowl 2": 0.50}
    else:
        likelihood = {"Bowl 1": 0.25,
                      "Bowl 2": 0.50}

    # Bayes update
    for bowl in posterior.index:
        posterior[bowl] *= likelihood[bowl]

    _ = posterior.normalize() # _ suppresses printing

    history.append(posterior["Bowl 1"])
```

```{python, echo=TRUE, results='hide'}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Plot the posterior probability after each observation
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8,4))

ax.plot(range(1, n+1), history, linewidth=2)

ax.set_xlabel("Number of Cookies Observed")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$P(\mathrm{Bowl\ 1}\mid\mathrm{Data})$")
ax.set_title("Bayesian Updating as Data Accumulate")

ax.grid(True)

plt.show()
```

Every vanilla cookie increases the odds in favor of Bowl 1 by a factor of 1.5,
while every chocolate cookie decreases the odds by a factor of 0.5. Since Bowl 1
generates vanilla cookies much more frequently than chocolate cookies, the
cumulative evidence overwhelmingly favors Bowl 1, causing the posterior
probability to approach 1.

If you draw a vanilla cookie the likelihood ratio is 1.5.

$$\frac{P(V|\text{Bowl 1})}{P(V|\text{Bowl 2})}=\frac{0.75}{0.5}=1.5$$

If you observe a chocolate cookie, the likelihood ratio is 0.5.

$$\frac{P(C|\text{Bowl 1})}{P(C|\text{Bowl 2})}=\frac{0.25}{0.5}=0.5$$

Bowl 1 produces many more vanilla cookies than chocolate cookies.

What would happen if the vanilla and chocolate cookies in each bowl were the
same.

To test this, I make the weights equal:

```text
# Generate a random sequence of observed cookies
cookies = random.choices(
    population=["vanilla", "chocolate"],
    weights=[0.5, 0.5],
    k=n
)
```

```{python,echo=FALSE,results='hide'}
from empiricaldist import Pmf
import random
random.seed(8675309)

# -----------------------------
# Prior probabilities
# -----------------------------
prior = Pmf([0.5, 0.5], ["Bowl 1", "Bowl 2"])

# Assume Bowl 1 is the true bowl
n = 100

# Generate a random sequence of observed cookies
cookies = random.choices(
    population=["vanilla", "chocolate"],
    weights=[0.5, 0.5],
    k=n
)

posterior = prior.copy()
history = []

for cookie in cookies:

    if cookie == "vanilla":
        likelihood = {"Bowl 1": 0.75,
                      "Bowl 2": 0.50}
    else:
        likelihood = {"Bowl 1": 0.25,
                      "Bowl 2": 0.50}

    # Bayes update
    for bowl in posterior.index:
        posterior[bowl] *= likelihood[bowl]

    _ = posterior.normalize() # _ suppresses printing

    history.append(posterior["Bowl 1"])

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Plot the posterior probability after each observation
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8,4))

ax.plot(range(1, n+1), history, linewidth=2)

ax.set_xlabel("Number of Cookies Observed")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$P(\mathrm{Bowl\ 1}\mid\mathrm{Data})$")
ax.set_title("Bayesian Updating as Data Accumulate")

ax.grid(True)

plt.show()
```

Here the data are generated from a 50/50 bowl, the same distribution as Bowl 2.
Consequently, the evidence increasingly favors Bowl 2, causing the posterior
probability of Bowl 1 to approach zero.

## Summary

In this lecture, we introduced Bayesian updating as a recursive process for
learning from data. Beginning with a prior distribution, we incorporated new
observations one at a time to obtain an updated posterior distribution. We saw
that the posterior becomes the prior for the next observation, allowing our
beliefs to evolve as additional evidence accumulates.

Using probability mass functions and the cookie bowl example, we demonstrated
that Bayesian inference is both conceptually intuitive and computationally
straightforward and naturally suited to sequential learning.

In the next lecture, we will take a closer look at the two ingredients that
drive this updating process—the prior distribution and the likelihood—and
examine how they combine to determine the posterior distribution.

\begin{keyidea}
Bayesian inference is not about making a single calculation, it's about
continuously updating our beliefs as new evidence becomes available.
\end{keyidea}
